title: How to Not Get Mauled on Your Hike This Summer
gist: A York University study of aggressive-wildlife reports from Canadian national parks, published in Frontiers in Conservation Science, found elk — not bears — are responsible for the most aggressive encounters with parkgoers (62%), driven by territorial behavior during mating and calving seasons and elk treating humans as threats when predators are nearby.

themes.theme: Elk, not bears, are the top wildlife threat in Canadian parks

insights.claim: Elk were involved in 62 percent of aggressive wildlife encounters with parkgoers identified in a York University study of Canadian national park incident reports, far outpacing grizzly bears (14 percent) and black bears (13 percent).
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Corrects a common public misperception (bears as the top hiking danger) with hard incident-rate data — useful for park-safety messaging, insurance/liability risk modeling, and outdoor-recreation-adjacent public health planning.
insights.quote: Elk, which were involved in 62 percent of aggressive encounters
insights.locator: The animal most likely to threaten parkgoers?

insights.claim: Conservation biologists from York University in the United Kingdom analyzed reports of aggressive wildlife spanning activities from relaxed nature walks to extreme sports in Canadian national parks, publishing the results in Frontiers in Conservation Science.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Establishes the study's scope (activity-type breadth) as the basis for the risk ranking, useful context for anyone trying to replicate or extend the analysis to other park systems.

insights.claim: Elk become significantly more territorial and aggressive toward humans during their mating and calving seasons, and they exhibit a 'love-hate relationship' with humans — congregating near people when actual predators are nearby, but treating humans as predators themselves when they feel threatened.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Gives the causal mechanism behind the headline statistic, which translates into actionable, season-specific guidance (avoid elk during mating/calving windows) for park visitors and park-safety programs alike.
insights.quote: They also have a love-hate relationship with humans, congregating near them when actual predators are near and treating them as predators themselves when they feel threatened
insights.locator: Elk become a lot more territorial
